role_summary: Co-founder of the Atlanta Student Movement and co-author of the 1960 Appeal for Human Rights; later Atlanta NAACP chapter president.
ai_reading: King's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 144), the Mississippi SNCC organizer, with C. T. Vivian (entry 134, the SCLC figure who taught nonviolent direct action in the South) close behind at 133. The cluster reads King as a movement-bridge voice between the Atlanta sit-in tradition he co-founded and both the Mississippi voter-registration cohort and the SCLC direct-action school; the embedding does not pull him into a strict local-Atlanta or Atlanta-University-Center cluster the cultural framing of him would have predicted. On the Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing axis his position of +0.52 reads his testimony as more secular than the 1960 Atlanta sit-ins' deeply church-anchored organizing tradition would predict, possibly an artifact of his post-sit-ins political and organizational career (NAACP chapter presidency, school-superintendent advocacy) dominating the vector signature over the early church-based protest framing.
biographical_paragraph: Lonnie C. King launched the Atlanta Student Movement in early 1960 and co-authored the Appeal for Human Rights, the published statement that opened the Atlanta movement's account of segregation in the city [src: 2]. On March 15, 1960, approximately two hundred students initiated coordinated sit-ins across Atlanta; by 1960-1961 the boycotts had cost segregated businesses roughly twenty million dollars in lost revenue [src: 2]. The Committee on Appeal for Human Rights that King and colleagues established coordinated the movement; by March 6, 1961, the effort had desegregated more than three hundred Atlanta restaurants and led to the rehiring of approximately six hundred African American employees who had been fired in retaliation [src: 2]. He later served as the Atlanta NAACP chapter president and advocated for the hiring of the city's first African American school superintendent [src: 2]. King was born August 30, 1936 in Arlington, Georgia, and died March 5, 2019 at eighty-two [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 80 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Lonnie C. King oral history
